items.mistake: Reporting training RMSE or MAE as final model quality
items.fix: Compute metrics on validation (for selection) and test (for final report) only. Training metrics are optimistically biased.
items.why: The model was fit to minimize error on training data — test RMSE/MAE estimate generalization.
items.example: Train RMSE = 0.8, test RMSE = 2.1 — publishing 0.8 overstates performance. Report 2.1 on the held-out split.

items.mistake: Comparing RMSE from one run to MAE from another without context
items.fix: Use the same metric on the same test split when comparing models. Report both RMSE and MAE if stakeholders need both views.
items.why: RMSE ≥ MAE always; the numbers are not interchangeable and rank models differently when error distributions differ.
items.example: Model A: test MAE=5, RMSE=7. Model B: test MAE=5.2, RMSE=6.1 — MAE ties A as winner; RMSE favors B.

items.mistake: Treating MSE and RMSE as different optima
items.fix: When discussing rankings, note RMSE = √MSE — same model order. Convert test MSE to RMSE for reporting, not as a separate objective.
items.why: √ is monotonic on non-negative values; argmin MSE = argmin RMSE.
items.example: Test MSE 100 vs 121 → RMSE 10 vs 11. The better model is the same; only the scale changed.

items.mistake: Scoring test data that leaked into training features
items.fix: Enforce train/val/test split discipline before any metric. Fit scalers and feature selectors on train only.
items.why: Leakage deflates RMSE/MAE artificially — metrics look good but fail in production.
items.example: Normalizing with full-dataset mean before split → test RMSE looks great; redeploy on new data and RMSE jumps.
